findings.title: 2x2 fast-path kernels never exercised with a correlated (off-diagonal) S
findings.description: mahalanobis2x2/gaussianScore2x2 are used exclusively in ImmEstimator::gate() and ImmEstimator::logLikelihood() on the Position2D fast path (ImmEstimator.cpp:503-525, 547-568). Every IMM Position2D test builds S from diagonal inputs only: measurement covariance is always Eigen::Matrix2d::Identity()*var and mode covariances are Identity(5,5) (tests/estimation/test_imm_estimator.cpp AnyModeGateAcceptsNearAnyMode, LogLikelihoodIsModeWeightedMixture..., UpdateShrinksPositionCovariance). With a diagonal S the closed-form cross term (S(0,1)+S(1,0))*y0*y1 is identically zero, so a sign error or index swap in that term is invisible. Grep confirms no test references gaussianScore2x2/mahalanobis2x2/gaussianScore/luInverseDet anywhere, and there is no test asserting the 2x2 kernel equals the generic LU path.
findings.why_it_matters: For the CoordinatedTurn mode in an IMM, F couples px to both vx and vy (CoordinatedTurn.cpp F(0,2..3), F(1,2..3)), so P(0,1) between px and py becomes non-zero after every predict — the operational S at the IMM gate/logLikelihood for a maneuvering target is genuinely correlated. A bug in the closed-form cross term would silently mis-score/mis-gate exactly those measurements, corrupting data association (wrong track-to-measurement assignment) with no unit test failing.
findings.suggested_test: Add a test that builds a 2x2 S with a real off-diagonal (e.g. S=[[4,1.5],[1.5,9]]) and a non-aligned innovation y, then asserts gaussianScore2x2/mahalanobis2x2 equal the generic gaussianScore/y^T S^-1 y to ~1e-12; and an IMM gate()/logLikelihood() case where the mode covariance has px-py correlation.

verdict.reasoning:
The claim's central factual premise — that the 2x2 fast-path kernels are "never exercised with a correlated (off-diagonal) S" — is FALSE, but its deeper concern (that a cross-term sign error would go uncaught) is only partially refuted, so I land on UNCERTAIN.

WHERE I SEARCHED
- grep for gaussianScore2x2/mahalanobis2x2/gaussianScore/luInverseDet/GaussianScore across tests/: only hit is tests/association/test_gating.cpp using an unrelated free function mahalanobisDistance. No test names the 2x2 kernels; no kernel-vs-generic-LU equivalence unit test exists. This half of the claim is TRUE.
- The direct IMM unit tests (tests/estimation/test_imm_estimator.cpp) do build S from diagonal inputs only: positionMeas() sets m.covariance = Matrix2d::Identity()*var (line 24) and the hand-built mode covariances are Identity(5,5) (lines 256, 310). So AnyModeGateAcceptsNearAnyMode / LogLikelihoodIsModeWeightedMixture / UpdateShrinksPositionCovariance all feed a diagonal S — the cross term (S01+S10)*y0*y1 is identically zero there. This part of the claim is also TRUE.

WHY "NEVER EXERCISED WITH CORRELATED S" IS FALSE
- gate()/logLikelihood() take the fast2d path whenever z.model == Position2D (ImmEstimator.cpp:503-525, 547-568), independent of which motion modes are configured.
- ARPA and EO-IR adapters emit Position2D (adapters/arpa/ArpaAdapter.cpp:81,144; adapters/eoir/EoIrAdapter.cpp:67) built via makeMeasurementFromRelativeBearing -> projectRangeBearingToEnu (core/types/MeasurementBuilders.cpp:71-81), which produces a rotated range/bearing covariance whose off-diagonal is nonzero for any non-cardinal bearing. So R itself is correlated, before P even contributes.
- These measurements flow through ImmEstimator::gate/logLikelihood in asserted scenario tests: GnnAssociator calls estimator->gate() and -logLikelihood() (core/association/GnnAssociator.cpp:38-41), and tests/sim/test_bus_imm3_comparison.cpp runs an ImmEstimator through GNN on the runBusManeuvering scenario (AIS+ARPA+EO-IR, correlated ARPA/EO R) with an assertion (line 71). PMBM/MHT also call estimator_.logLikelihood/gate on Position2D radar (core/pmbm/PmbmTracker.cpp:290,467,786,792; tests/benchmark/test_imazu22_scenario_run.cpp uses config imm_cv_ct_mht with a firm id_switches bound at line 161). So the 2x2 kernel IS exercised with a genuinely correlated S under assertions — the auditor missed this.

WHY IT IS ONLY UNCERTAIN, NOT FULLY REFUTED
I cannot point to one assertion and prove it flips on a cross-term sign error:
- The most direct IMM scenario tests (tests/scenario/test_filter_comparison.cpp ManeuveringTarget, Maneuvering3ModeIMM) run IMM through GNN but place NO EXPECT on the IMM output — they only fprintf its metrics. They would not catch anything.
- test_bus_imm3_comparison.cpp asserts only the soft comparative "IMM beats CV on OSPA OR id_switches" over a single-target scenario, where association is trivial; a mis-scored d2 near a well-tracked lone target is unlikely to reject the true measurement, so a sign flip may not move the aggregate past the assertion.
- The strong multi-target correlated-S firm-assertion tests (imazu imm_cv_ct_mht id_switch bound; harbor/philos/haxr PMBM OSPA replays) are exactly where a sign flip could plausibly break an assertion, but the bounds carry margin and these are fixture-gated, so I cannot confirm the breakage flips them.

Net: the literal "never exercised with correlated S" is refuted by the ARPA/EO/PMBM path; the residual "no test reliably catches a subtle cross-term sign error" has merit but is not established. Coverage exists (exercise under assertions) yet its catching power for this specific breakage is questionable — UNCERTAIN.

findings.title: IMM softUpdate normalized-likelihood branch (V_j gate volume) is never covered
findings.description: The ctx.p_d>0 && ctx.gate_threshold>0 branch computes the per-mode gate volume V_j = c_d*gamma^d*sqrt(det) with c_d = 2 for d==1 and pi for d==2 (lines 395-405). This path is production-active: JpdaAssociator sets out.p_d (JpdaAssociator.cpp:76/81) and Tracker::softUpdate forwards ctx (Tracker.cpp:250). But every ImmEstimator::softUpdate unit test calls it with a default-constructed PdaContext (p_d=0 -> proxy branch): tests/estimation/test_imm_estimator.cpp SoftUpdateWithSingleConfidentMeasurement... and SoftUpdateActuallyMovesState... pass only 4 args. And tests/scenario/test_multi_seed_sweep.cpp runs JPDA (runBatched/softUpdate) only with EkfEstimator; the IMM runs use gnn/runStandard (hard update). So no unit or scenario test drives IMM+JPDA with p_d>0.
findings.why_it_matters: V_j depends on sqrt(det(S_j)) per mode, so the normalization changes the RELATIVE mode weighting when modes have different innovation covariances — exactly the maneuver-detection signal IMM exists for. A bug in c_d, gamma, or V_j would silently skew mode probabilities under IMM+JPDA, degrading maneuvering-target tracking, and nothing would catch it.
findings.suggested_test: Call ImmEstimator::softUpdate with a PdaContext{p_d=0.9, gate_threshold=9.0} on a two-mode track and assert the resulting mode probabilities against a hand-computed V_j-normalized mixture; run one multi_seed_sweep case with IMM+JPDA.

findings.title: ParticleFilterEstimator::update lacks the R-validity guard that EKF/UKF/IMM all have
findings.description: EKF, UKF, and IMM each call isMeasurementCovariancePsd(z.covariance) at update entry and skip on failure (EkfEstimator.cpp:37, UkfEstimator.cpp:61, ImmEstimator.cpp:170 — the documented Phase 8 R3 fix against 'one bad NMEA frame poisoning a track'). ParticleFilterEstimator::update has no such guard and calls z.covariance.inverse() directly (line 79). PF::initiate guards via LLT, and PF::update early-returns when particles.cols()==0, so a bad-R birth is safe — but a track born with good R that later receives a measurement carrying the documented empty 0x0 'no uncertainty' sentinel or a non-PSD R reaches inverse() on an empty/degenerate matrix. tests/estimation/test_particle_filter_estimator.cpp exercises only good-R updates; PredictOnEmptyEnsembleIsNoOp covers the empty-ensemble path, not a bad-R update on a populated ensemble.
findings.why_it_matters: inverse() on a 0x0 matrix followed by y^T Rinv y (y is 2-dim) is a dimension mismatch -> assert in debug, UB in release; a non-PSD R produces NaN log-weights that silently corrupt or kill the track. The other estimators were explicitly hardened against this; PF is the inconsistent outlier for a wired (if non-default) estimator.
findings.suggested_test: Feed a populated PF track an update with z.covariance = Eigen::MatrixXd() (0x0) and with a negative-eigenvalue R; assert the update is a no-op (track state/weights unchanged) as EKF/UKF/IMM do.

findings.title: No end-to-end NEES/NIS consistency test on a real estimator run
findings.description: The Consistency machinery (NisCollector, computeNees) is thoroughly validated against synthetic IID Gaussian draws and hand-crafted BenchResults (tests/benchmark/test_consistency.cpp: NisFromIidGaussianResiduals, NeesFromKnownFilter, NisFittedAlpha, SingularSDropped). But no test runs an actual EKF/UKF/IMM/PF over a synthetic truth+noise scenario and asserts the estimator's own reported covariance is statistically calibrated (NEES/NIS inside the chi-square band). Scenario tests assert accuracy/identity only: tests/scenario/test_filter_comparison.cpp and test_multi_seed_sweep.cpp check mean_ospa, final_track_count, id_switches — never covariance magnitude vs empirical error. Estimator unit tests assert covariance only qualitatively (EXPECT_LT/GT shrink/grow).
findings.why_it_matters: A covariance-calibration bug that leaves the mean correct — e.g. a wrong process-noise scale, a dropped PDAF spread term, or a Joseph-form regression — would keep RMSE/OSPA green while the reported P is silently over- or under-confident. That corrupts gating (too tight/loose), CPA collision-risk uncertainty, and PMBM existence weighting. The tool to catch it exists but is never pointed at a live estimator.
findings.suggested_test: Run each estimator over a seeded CV/CT scenario with known R and Q, feed InnovationEvents into NisCollector (and BenchResult into computeNees), and assert the mean NIS/NEES lands inside the reported 95% band.

units.coverage_notes:
WELL COVERED: (1) Measurement Jacobians — RangeBearing2D is checked against finite differences (tests/estimation/test_measurement_models.cpp JacobianMatchesFiniteDifference) plus analytic pins; Bearing2D is pinned analytically (Bearing2DPredictAndJacobian) and its single row equals the finite-diff-checked RangeBearing bearing row; offset-sensor variants and state-dimension adaptation covered. (2) SigmaPoints robustness fully covered: weights-sum-to-one, mean & covariance reconstruction, non-PD rank-1 fallback, and indefinite/negative-eigenvalue clamping all stay finite (test_sigma_points.cpp). (3) CoordinatedTurn omega=0 CV-limit branch AND finite-omega rotation both asserted, plus propagate reads omega from state, processNoise diagonal, and UKF>EKF on an arc (test_coordinated_turn.cpp, test_ukf_estimator.cpp BeatsEkfOnCoordinatedTurnQuarterArc). (4) IMM dt-scaled TPM: dt=1 short-circuit, pi^dt scaling, and the semigroup/composition property (ModeTransitionIsDtScaled, ModeTransitionComposesAcrossPredicts). (5) IMM any-mode gate and mode-weighted-mixture logLikelihood vs moment-matched baseline (but only with diagonal S — see finding 1). (6) UKF==EKF agreement on linear predict and linear update; UKF/EKF inner-filter equivalence inside IMM for linear h. (7) PSD/NaN guards on EKF/UKF/IMM update and initiate, and on IEstimator::gate/logLikelihood defaults (fails-closed / returns -inf); empty 0x0 and non-PSD covariance both tested for EKF initiate. (8) StudentTNoiseModel scale formula (inlier/outlier/clamp) plus end-to-end EKF outlier resistance; GaussianNoiseModel always-1. (9) PF determinism-by-seed, ESS-triggered systematic resampling, CT per-particle propagate, 5-state sizing, empty-ensemble no-op. (10) NIS/NEES collector internals validated with IID draws incl. Wilson-Hilferty band, fitted alpha/beta, singular-S drop, low-sample suppression. dt<=0 predict guard exists in all estimators (EKF/UKF/PF/IMM) and is defensively correct but has no direct no-op test on a populated track — judged low risk (out-of-order dt is prevented upstream by the reorder buffer) so not filed as a finding. COULD NOT DETERMINE: whether the fixture-gated replay tests (replay/test_philos_*, test_haxr_*) drive IMM with correlated-S Position2D or IMM+JPDA; they assert end-to-end OSPA only, so even if they run they would not pin the 2x2 cross-term or the softUpdate V_j normalization, and they SKIP without fixtures. No direct absolute-value check exists on the generic gaussianScore/luInverseDet output, but it is exercised indirectly through IMM/IEstimator logLikelihood and association behavior tests.</coverage_notes>
</invoke>

findings.title: Same-scan two-sensor fusion of ONE target is untested end-to-end
findings.description: The point-target PMBM update builds a single (n+m)x m cost matrix and assigns at most ONE measurement per Bernoulli per scan (cost matrix + Murty at PmbmTracker.cpp:760-840). So when two sensors (e.g. radar + AIS/cooperative) report the SAME vessel in one processBatch, one return updates the Bernoulli and the other births a competing new-target Bernoulli; the design relies on mergeBernoulliDuplicates (PmbmTracker.cpp:1930, default bhattacharyya_merge_threshold=1.0) to collapse them into one track. Every multi-sensor fusion scenario test DELIBERATELY avoids this: measurements are staggered into separate scans with explicit comments 'no two-in-one-scan competition' (tests/pmbm/test_pmbm_remote_track_fusion.cpp:192 and :321; tests/pmbm/test_pmbm_sensor_activity.cpp:1133 FuseOneTrackStableThroughCoopDropout). tests/pmbm/test_pmbm_phase8.cpp:344 ('two simultaneous measurements') uses two DIFFERENT targets, not one target seen by two sensors. tests/pmbm/test_pmbm_clutter_feed.cpp:130-131 and :162-171 explicitly state the collapse case 'cannot be isolated in a unit test'. mergeBernoulliDuplicates is unit-tested only on hand-constructed Bernoullis, never as the outcome of a two-in-one-scan processBatch.
findings.why_it_matters: This is the library's central promise (fuse AIS/radar/EO-IR into a single authoritative track) on its most realistic input: sensors reporting simultaneously in one fused batch. If the two same-scan births land far enough apart (e.g. a sensor-bias-sized offset between the radar plot and the AIS position) their Bhattacharyya distance exceeds the merge threshold and they do NOT merge, shipping a persistent duplicate track (or ID churn as the assignment flips which sensor 'owns' the Bernoulli) per multi-sensor vessel. A regression here is silent — no test asserts one-track-out for simultaneous multi-sensor detection of a single target.
findings.suggested_test: Drive processBatch({z_radar_targetX, z_ais_targetX}) in a SINGLE scan (same timestamp) for one moving target on default config; assert exactly one Confirmed track over the run, stable track_id (no ID switch), and position accuracy vs truth. Add a variant where the two sensor returns carry a small realistic offset (e.g. 5-15 m) to exercise the merge threshold boundary.

verdict.reasoning:
I confirm the auditor's claim: no test exercises same-scan two-sensor fusion of ONE target end-to-end through processBatch, and none would catch a duplicate-track / ID-churn regression from the two competing same-scan births failing to merge.

Source verified (core/pmbm/PmbmTracker.cpp:760-840): the point-target update builds one (n+m)×m cost matrix and runs Murty, assigning at most one measurement per Bernoulli per scan; a second same-target measurement in the same batch lands on a new-target diagonal row and births a competing Bernoulli. Collapse is left to mergeBernoulliDuplicates (Bhattacharyya threshold).

The design intent and the untested residual are stated explicitly in tests/pmbm/test_pmbm_clutter_feed.cpp:162-178: an earlier guard against folding two same-scan distinct-claim Bernoullis was REVERTED precisely because "a fused batch is multi-sensor, so one physical target detected by two sensors ... legitimately yields two distinct-claim Bernoullis that MUST merge; the guard would leave a duplicate track per vessel." So the merge is load-bearing, yet it is never verified as the outcome of a two-in-one-scan batch.

Coverage I searched and ruled out:
- Multi-sensor fusion scenario tests deliberately stagger measurements into separate scans: tests/pmbm/test_pmbm_remote_track_fusion.cpp:191-192 ("Staggered so no two-in-one-scan competition") and :320-321, with confirmedCount==1 assertions (:230,:246,:255,:348) that therefore never hit the same-scan path; tests/pmbm/test_pmbm_sensor_activity.cpp:1132-1133 (FuseOneTrack..., "no two-in-one-scan competition; they gate to the same Bernoulli").
- tests/pmbm/test_pmbm_clutter_feed.cpp:130-133 TwoTargetsBothReturnsClaimed uses TWO DIFFERENT targets 25 m apart and explicitly says "the collapse case cannot be isolated in a unit test."
- Bhattacharyya merge is unit-tested only on hand-constructed Bernoullis seeded via mutableDensityForTesting() with a far-away single measurement to trigger the fold: tests/pmbm/test_pmbm_phase8.cpp:173 (BhattacharyyaMergeKeepsOlderIdAndDeletesYounger, near-coincident 0.0/0.1) and :237 (merge-off). These never run a two-sensor processBatch, and cover only the near-coincident (merge-succeeds) and merge-disabled cases — not the sensor-bias-offset case where distance exceeds threshold and the duplicate persists.
- The phase8 same-scan multi-measurement batches ({z_hi,z_lo} at :369,:500,:540 and the T4 per-sensor model test at :344) all use TWO DIFFERENT targets (e.g. 0,0 vs 100,100) and assert bernoullis.size()==2 — the opposite of one-target-two-sensors.
- PDA tests (tests/pmbm/test_pmbm_pda_soft_update.cpp:70,:130) feed two same-scan measurements but they are one established track + clutter (clutter-pull resistance), default-off PDA branch, no single-track-out assertion for two-sensor same-target.
- The canonical integration example tests/integration/test_full_stack_pipeline.cpp uses the GNN/MHT Tracker with a single AIS stream, not the PMBM same-scan two-sensor path.

No PMBM scenario/replay test feeds two sensors reporting the same vessel at the same timestamp and asserts exactly one output track, so the specific silent regression (two same-scan births offset by a sensor bias exceeding the Bhattacharyya merge threshold → persistent duplicate or ID churn per multi-sensor vessel) would go uncaught.

findings.title: Velocity-runaway innovation gate (#25 Phase 2b) has no behavioral test
findings.description: The innov_gate_max_m guard is a three-part mechanism: the true raw position innovation is computed on each detected cell (PmbmTracker.cpp:807-818), the gate fires when it exceeds innov_gate_max_m on the assigned Bernoulli (PmbmTracker.cpp:973-976), and applyInnovationGate clamps/resets velocity+turn-rate and inflates their covariance on the moment-matched state AND every IMM mode (PmbmTracker.cpp:2326-2367, config innov_gate_action / innov_gate_velocity_var_floor). A constrained grep of tests (excluding tests/fixtures) finds these symbols only in tests/benchmark/test_config.cpp (config parsing) — no test ever triggers the gate or checks the clamped state.
findings.why_it_matters: This is a safety guard that exists specifically to prevent a corrupted measurement from throwing a track's velocity/COG off (a wrong-track failure mode that feeds CPA/collision-risk downstream). It is default-off, but a consumer who enables it gets zero protection against a silent regression: if the innovation computation or the clamp no-ops, the runaway it was built to stop ships wrong SOG/COG and nothing catches it.
findings.suggested_test: With innov_gate_max_m set (e.g. 50 m) and kVelocityReset action, run a track to steady velocity, then feed one grossly displaced position measurement that wins its assignment; assert the accepted position moved but velocity/turn-rate were reset and their variance raised to the floor (and IMM modes likewise), and that the next scan does not show runaway SOG. Add a control at innov_gate_max_m<=0 asserting byte-identical (no clamp).

findings.title: JPDA joint-event overflow -> greedy hard-assignment fallback is untested
findings.description: Two coupled overflow paths have no coverage. (1) enumerateJointEvents(V, max_events) returns {} on overflow (core/association/JointEvents.cpp:24,52) but tests/association/test_joint_events.cpp only calls the unbounded overload — the max_events cap and empty-on-overflow contract are never tested. (2) When enumeration overflows, JpdaAssociator degrades the whole gating cluster to a greedy mutual-exclusion hard assignment (betas -> 0/1) and sets overflow_fallback=true (JpdaAssociator.cpp:161-185); a constrained grep finds overflow_fallback asserted in no non-fixture test. Also the degenerate normalization fallback (sum<=0 or non-finite -> all-miss, JpdaAssociator.cpp:216-220) is untested.
findings.why_it_matters: The cap (1e6 events) is the safety valve for exactly the deployment reality-check case — a large real-clutter gating cluster that would otherwise OOM. On such scans the entire cluster's association is decided by the greedy branch instead of soft JPDA. A bug in the density-sorted greedy pick, or in the empty-signal handshake between enumerateJointEvents and the associator, would silently produce wrong association on the hardest (dense-clutter) scans with no test to catch it.
findings.suggested_test: Unit-test enumerateJointEvents(V, small_max) on a V that exceeds the cap, asserting it returns empty. Separately, exercise the JPDA greedy branch directly (either by making the cap injectable or with a fully-gated cluster sized to overflow) and assert overflow_fallback==true, betas are 0/1 mutual-exclusion by descending density, and each measurement/track used at most once.

findings.title: JPDA estimator-routed betas (production path) are executed but never asserted
findings.description: Production wires JPDA through Tracker.cpp:142/221 as associate(tracks, batch, &estimator_), taking the estimator branch (JpdaAssociator.cpp:99-101: estimator->gate + exp(estimator->logLikelihood)). Every JpdaAssociator unit test in tests/association/test_jpda_associator.cpp calls associate WITHOUT an estimator, exercising only the inline single-Gaussian density path (JpdaAssociator.cpp:103-116). The scenario tests that do route through an estimator (tests/scenario/test_jpda_comparison.cpp) print OSPA/id_switch diagnostics to stderr with no EXPECT/ASSERT.
findings.why_it_matters: The estimator path is the one that matters for IMM: it substitutes the mode-mixture likelihood for the single-Gaussian surrogate, which is the whole reason the parameter exists. With an EKF the two paths nearly coincide (so the asserted inline tests approximate it), but IMM-mixture betas are unverified — a regression in the estimator-routed likelihood normalization would go unnoticed.
findings.suggested_test: Add a JpdaAssociator test that passes an estimator (EKF first for a golden check against the inline path within tolerance, then IMM) and asserts betas/beta_0 values for a small hand-computable 2-track/3-measurement gate, including the no-detection (beta_0) column summing to one.

findings.title: PPP component cap (max_ppp_components) keep-top-weighted behavior untested
findings.description: pruneAndNormalise caps the PPP at max_ppp_components (default 200) via partial_sort keeping the highest-weight components (PmbmTracker.cpp:1284-1293). max_ppp_components appears in no non-fixture test; no test drives the PPP past the cap to confirm the strongest components survive. The comment notes the cap only matters on long replays under measurement-driven birth, where the PPP grows unbounded otherwise.
findings.why_it_matters: If the sort direction or resize were wrong, the cap would drop the strongest undetected-target birth candidates instead of the weakest, silently suppressing legitimate births (missed tracks) only on long real replays — invisible to the short synthetic scenarios. Severity is low because the analogous MBM hypothesis cap (max_global_hypotheses) IS exercised (tests/pmbm/test_pmbm_scenario.cpp, test_pmbm_remote_track_fusion.cpp), so the partial_sort-keep-top pattern is validated once, just not for the PPP copy.
findings.suggested_test: Set max_ppp_components small (e.g. 3), inject >3 PPP components with distinct known weights, run one predict/prune, and assert exactly the top-3-by-weight survive.

units.coverage_notes: Well covered: (1) Murty K-best — uniqueness on square 3x3, non-decreasing costs on 5x5, full brute-force enumeration of all 6 permutations of a 3x3, forbidden-cell honoring, stop-at-feasible-count, rectangular surplus-cols first-assignment + monotonicity, and K<=0/empty edges (tests/association/test_murty.cpp). I also hand-verified the Miller-Stone-Cox partition is exhaustive and non-duplicating even for the (n+m)x m surplus-ROW shape PMBM actually feeds, so the thin direct rectangular test is backed by correctness of the algorithm plus heavy PMBM integration (MurtyPicksMatchedAssignmentAsBestChild, MixtureWeightsSumToOneAfterUpdate). (2) Hungarian — square min-sum, rectangular more-cols-than-rows, forbidden cells, and the degenerate all-inf-row no-hang pathology (tests/association/test_hungarian.cpp); the more-ROWS-than-cols surplus path (rows->-1) is only exercised transitively via Murty/PMBM, which is adequate. (3) JPDA — soft betas, ungated->0 beta, column+beta_0=1 normalization incl no-detection event, empty inputs, and per-sensor mode (single-sensor==scalar, per-measurement lambda_C isolation, out-of-coverage no-miss, homogeneous vs heterogeneous batch p_d) (tests/association/test_jpda_associator.cpp). (4) JointEvents — no-measurement single-empty-event, gating restriction, mutual-exclusion feasibility count (tests/association/test_joint_events.cpp). (5) PMBM — extensive: predict decay/propagation/zero-dt, misdetection existence math, PPP birth/decay-by-(1-Pd), r_min and hypothesis-weight-min pruning, MBM hypothesis cap, log-sum-exp normalization + all-(-inf) uniform keep-alive (exercised via pruneAndNormalise), Bhattacharyya merge on/off (older-id survivor), adaptive birth r_new=lambda ratio + out-of-range fallback, per-sensor Pd/lambda_C differentiation, PDA soft-detected branch, sensor-activity miss model + hypothesis-order consistency, source-aware misdetection/identity gate, clutter-feed claim credit, per-track view rebuild idempotence/match-flat, RTS smoothing, TPMBM trajectory window, replay determinism, and lifecycle events. Recycling is NOT a concern — no recycle-to-PPP path exists in the source (attention item does not apply). Could not fully determine: whether any long-replay fixture test drives the PPP past max_ppp_components or ever trips the JPDA 1e6-event cap (fixtures present but not built/run here) — but overflow_fallback is asserted in zero source-level tests regardless. Not built/executed anything (read-only tree).

findings.title: ID-never-reused invariant is not asserted across any delete->create cycle
findings.description: CLAUDE.md invariant #5 and both class docs promise stable TrackIds that are never reused after deletion. The only tests that touch id allocation (tests/tracking/test_track_manager.cpp::AssignsMonotonicStableIds asserting id1=1,id2=2; ResolvesIdsCorrectlyAfterMiddleErase which only checks post-erase lookups; tests/pipeline/test_mht_cross_merge.cpp asserting the surviving id stays 1u) never delete a track and then add a NEW one to check the fresh id is strictly greater than every id ever issued and never equals a deleted id. I grepped tests/ for 'reuse/never reuse/next_id' (only a PMBM comment match), and every mgr.add(...) call site in tests/tracking and tests/pipeline. The invariant currently holds only because both allocators are bare monotonic counters (next_id_++ / next_external_id_++). A regression that recycled a freed id/slot (e.g. deriving the id from tracks_.size(), or resetting the counter when the set empties) would pass the entire suite.
findings.why_it_matters: Reused ids silently corrupt downstream identity: OSPA/id-switch metrics, operator display continuity, and any consumer keying on track_id would conflate a dead vessel with a new one. This is an explicit architecture invariant with zero guarding test.
findings.suggested_test: Add a TrackManager test: add 3 tracks, delete_misses to remove one, add another, assert new id > max(prior ids) and != the deleted id; and an MhtTracker test that lets a tree die (score/existence delete) then spawns a new tree and asserts the new externalId exceeds the dead one.

findings.title: SPRT confirm-threshold tests use sprt_alpha == sprt_beta, so an alpha/beta swap or asymmetry regression is invisible
findings.description: t_confirm = log((1 - sprt_beta)/max(sprt_alpha,1e-12)). The only SPRT tests (tests/pipeline/test_mht_sprt_confirm.cpp) set sprt_alpha = sprt_beta = 0.01, which makes T_confirm = ln(0.99/0.01) symmetric in the two parameters. CleanTargetConfirmsUnderSprt and SingleDetectionDoesNotConfirm would both still pass if the formula accidentally swapped alpha and beta, dropped the (1-beta) numerator, or otherwise became asymmetric, because the numeric threshold is unchanged when the two inputs are equal. No test varies alpha vs beta to pin the direction/magnitude of the threshold.
findings.why_it_matters: SPRT is default-off today but is documented as the JIPDA-direction confirmation path; a miscalibrated threshold would confirm/reject at the wrong false-track rate whenever it is enabled, and the current tests cannot catch the most likely coding error (parameter swap).
findings.suggested_test: Parametrize with asymmetric values (e.g. alpha=0.001, beta=0.2 vs alpha=0.2, beta=0.001), assert the number of scans-to-confirm changes monotonically with alpha, and directly compare tree score against the hand-computed ln((1-beta)/alpha).

findings.title: The M-of-N confirmation branch in MhtTracker is never asserted to promote a track
findings.description: The confirm gate falls through to M-of-N (countHitsInWindow(leaf, confirm_hits_window) >= confirm_hits_needed) only when use_ipda_lifecycle=false AND use_sprt_confirm=false. The default config has IPDA on, and the SPRT tests set sprt=true. The one test that reaches the pure M-of-N path (tests/pipeline/test_mht_tracker.cpp::ObserveBundleFullWeightWhenNoTreeClaims, ipda=false/visibility=false) asserts nothing about track status. So the confirm_hits_needed/confirm_hits_window wiring at the MhtTracker level (the >= comparison and which config fields feed it) has no assertion; only the underlying TrackTree::countHitsInWindow primitive is unit-tested (tests/tracking/test_track_tree.cpp:185-189).
findings.why_it_matters: A regression swapping confirm_hits_needed with confirm_hits_window, or an off-by-one in the comparison, would mis-time (or never issue) Confirmed status on the documented default confirmation mode without any red test. It is a non-default ablation path today, which caps the severity.
findings.suggested_test: MhtTracker test with use_ipda_lifecycle=false, use_sprt_confirm=false, confirm_hits_needed=2/window=3: feed a clean single target and assert Tentative before the 2nd hit and Confirmed after, and that a hit followed by two misses inside the window does not confirm.

units.coverage_notes:
WELL COVERED (good news for synthesis): (1) Stale-input guard for both single-hyp Tracker and MhtTracker, including the equal-timestamp boundary (Tracker::EqualTimestampIsNotStale, MhtTracker::EqualTimestampScanIsNotStale), opt-out, and byte-identical parity vs a clean twin (test_tracker.cpp:214-284, test_mht_tracker.cpp:98-145). (2) ReorderBuffer drop policy is unusually thorough: unit tests plus tests/scenario/test_reorder_buffer_e2e.cpp verifies determinism under skew, monotonic drains, comfortable-window-drops-zero, and tight-window drop count matching an independently-computed ground-truth late count over crossing + AIS-dropout scenarios. Multimap equal-key ordering is deterministic (C++11) and exercised indirectly by the determinism tests. (3) MeasurementBuilders covariance composition is checked against hand-computed projectRangeBearingToEnu references, including the #16 heading-sigma quadrature composition, the config-floor clamp (floor only widens), per-pose-sigma widening, the zero-covariance sentinel + applyDefaultsIfEmpty path, no-datum/no-pose empty returns, and pose-at-timestamp selection. (4) batch-ordering: MhtTracker and PmbmTracker sort unsorted batches to match sorted input and are determinism-checked (test_batch_ordering.cpp). (5) TrackTree::pruneNScan depth semantics have two dedicated tests (KeepsBestTrunk, PreservesProtectedAncestorChain) plus MhtTracker smoke tests for n_scan_extension_when_protected and score_delta_threshold not exploding trees. (6) TrackManager lifecycle (confirm-on-consecutive-hits, coast-then-delete, middle-erase reindex, delete-fires-before-erase, null-sink safety) and the cross-tree duplicate-merge ID-stability (older id survives) are covered.

COULD NOT FULLY DETERMINE / notes: (a) TrackManager sink REENTRANCY (a sink calling back into add/recordMiss during onTrackDeleted, which fires before the erase+reindex at TrackManager.cpp:50-62) is neither tested nor documented as supported; if a consumer does it, the mid-erase vector/index mutation is a latent hazard, but it is not clearly an intended behavior so I did not raise it as a finding. (b) Asymmetry vs the base Tracker: MhtTracker/PmbmTracker sort batches internally but Tracker::processBatch does NOT (it uses scan.front().time as the single scan instant); this is consistent with its documented same-timestamp-batch precondition, so it is a precondition rather than a bug. (c) The pipeline's initiate-guard branches (canInitiateTrack rejecting lone Bearing2D, isMeasurementCovariancePsd rejecting non-PSD covariance before seeding) have only indirect/fixture-gated coverage (tests/replay/test_camera_bearing_loader.cpp) plus estimation-layer unit tests; a direct single-hyp pipeline assertion that a lone bearing / NaN-covariance measurement seeds no track is absent, but adapters validate at the edges (invariant #6) so I rated it below the bar. Fixture-gated replay tests may SKIP if data/ is absent.

findings.title: datumAxisRotation does not wrap longitude delta — antimeridian recenter rotates track velocity/covariance by a bogus ~360 deg
findings.description: datumAxisRotation computes delta_lon_rad = (n.lon_deg - o.lon_deg) * kDeg2Rad with no normalization to [-180,180]. When an auto-recenter fires with the old and new datum origins straddling the +/-180 meridian (e.g. old=+179.9, new=-179.8), the true convergence-angle delta of ~0.3 deg is computed as ~-359.7 deg. The resulting R rotates every track's velocity and position/covariance blocks (via shiftTracksOnDatumChange -> rotateVelocityInPlace/rotateCovarianceInPlace in core/tracking/DatumShift.cpp) by that bogus angle. Note the POSITION shift itself is antimeridian-safe (it goes through ECEF in Datum::toGeodetic/toEnu), so positions stay correct while COG and the error-ellipse orientation are silently corrupted — a hard-to-notice failure. tests/tracking/test_datum_shift.cpp and tests/scenario/test_datum_recenter_scenario.cpp only exercise 8.0->8.5E / 8.0->9.0E (small positive deltas); no test crosses the antimeridian or uses negative longitudes.
findings.why_it_matters: A recenter across the date line produces wrong track velocities (COG) and mis-oriented covariance for all live tracks, violating the accuracy/continuity expectations, with nothing to catch it. Operationally rare for the North Sea / Korea deployments in the memory, hence medium rather than high, but it is a present latent defect, not merely a missing test.
findings.suggested_test: Add a datumAxisRotation/shiftTracksOnDatumChange test with old_datum lon=+179.9 and new_datum lon=-179.8 (same lat) and assert the rotation angle equals ~+0.3deg*sin(lat), not ~-360deg; assert a track's velocity direction is essentially preserved.

findings.title: Projected covariance off-diagonal (ellipse orientation) never independently asserted at a non-cardinal bearing
findings.description: projectRangeBearingToEnu builds cov = J R J^T with J = [[sb, r*cb],[cb, -r*sb]]. Every direct test in tests/adapters/util/test_projection.cpp uses bearing = pi/2 or 0, where the analytic off-diagonal cov(0,1) is exactly 0 (and is asserted =0). The only non-cardinal case (GpsStdComposesWithExistingNoise, bearing=1.0) asserts only the DIFFERENCE (with_gps - base) off-diagonal is ~0, never base's off-diagonal value. tests/types/test_measurement_builders.cpp (RelativeBearingProducesEnuConsistentWithDirectProjection, rel_bearing=30deg) checks m.covariance(i,j) == projectRangeBearingToEnu(...).cov(i,j) — a tautology comparing the function to itself. So the sign/magnitude of the cross-correlation term for an off-axis bearing is never verified against an independent analytic value.
findings.why_it_matters: A sign error in J(0,1)/J(1,0)/J(1,1) flips the tilt of the error ellipse without changing the diagonal or the cardinal-bearing off-diagonal, so it survives every existing test. This is the main covariance path for radar/EOIR range-bearing measurements at arbitrary bearings; a mis-oriented ellipse corrupts gating, association, and fusion weighting for the common off-axis case -> wrong associations / wrong tracks.
findings.suggested_test: Call projectRangeBearingToEnu with bearing ~1.0 rad and assert cov(0,1)=cov(1,0) equals the hand-computed J R J^T off-diagonal (sb*cb*range_std^2 - r^2*cb*sb*angular_var) to tight tolerance.

findings.title: Bearing-bias outlier gate has no rejection test (only the position gate is covered)
findings.description: observe(BearingBiasPairObservation) has a Mahalanobis outlier gate (r*r > outlier_sigma^2 * S) that protects a converged EOIR bearing bias from a single bad AIS/coop-anchored pair. tests/bias/test_sensor_bias_estimator.cpp tests the POSITION outlier gate (PositionOutlierGateRejects, rejectedByOutlier==1) and bearing CONVERGENCE (BearingConvergesToTrueBias), but no test drives a large bearing residual through the bearing path to confirm it is rejected. rejectedByOutlier() is a shared counter, so even indirectly the bearing branch is unverified.
findings.why_it_matters: A regression in the bearing gate (e.g. wrong comparison direction or dropped S scaling) would let a single spoofed/mis-associated pair pull a published EOIR bearing bias, which flows into bias correction and shifts EOIR-derived bearings off truth — with no test going red.
findings.suggested_test: Feed ~20 clean bearing pairs to converge, then one pair with a ~90deg residual and assert bearingBias is essentially unchanged and rejectedByOutlier increments.

findings.title: Two NavInputGuard branches uncovered: derived-SOG low-speed flag and heading-delta wrap
findings.description: tests/own_ship/test_nav_input_guard.cpp covers low-SOG via velocity_is_valid=true, stale, position_jump, heading_jump, first-pose, and out-of-order. Two branches are unexercised: (1) the derived-SOG path (line 52-53: velocity_is_valid=false, dt>0, sog = step_m/dt below heading_min_sog_mps) that sets heading_unreliable_low_sog — the one position_jump test with sog_valid=false uses a huge step so this low branch never fires; (2) headingDeltaDeg's wrap arm (line 22-24, d>180 -> 360-d), e.g. heading 359->1 should be 2deg not 358deg. HeadingJumpFlagged only uses 0->90 (no wrap).
findings.why_it_matters: Low because the guard is diagnostic-only (invariant #6: it flags, never rewrites the pose, and core tracking does not consume NavHealth), so a bug here degrades operator warnings, not track output. Still, a broken heading-wrap would falsely raise heading_jump on every heading rollover near north.
findings.suggested_test: Add a case with velocity_is_valid=false and a small position step (~0.1 m/s) asserting heading_unreliable_low_sog=true and position_jump=false; add a heading 359->1 deg over 1 s asserting heading_step_deg~2 and heading_jump=false.

units.coverage_notes: WELL COVERED (good news for synthesis): (1) Bias sign conventions per observation kind are all sign-sensitive and would go red on a flip: GpsHeading (biasRad ~= K*kBiasTrue), Cog (EXPECT_GT 0), Magnetic (EXPECT_GT 0), AisArpa (SinglePairMovesBiasTowardTruth EXPECT_GT 0), BearingInnovation (biasRad ~= K*r), SensorBias position (true_b(3,-2) both axes) and bearing (0.02), plus the full-pipeline BearingBiasConvergence scenario reconstructs +kBiasTrue. The specific sign-flip concern in the prompt is NOT a gap. (2) HeadingBiasEstimator: all five MHS kinds have accept + gate-rejection tests (low-SOG, turning, null-variation noop, outlier), plus range/state-var/outlier gates on BearingInnovation, wrap-around, drift tracking, GPS floor, and publish/stale gating. (3) SensorBiasEstimator: convergence, per-key independence, position range+outlier gates, seed-anchoring semantics, and the full cross-sensor extractor suite (one-obs-per-key, same-source-id skip, Schmidt fold, deterministic key order, existence/cov/AIS eligibility gates, pinned-anchor recovery). (4) OwnShipProvider: exhaustive — empty/latest, poseAtOrBefore exact/earlier/nullopt, out-of-order insertion, equal-timestamp last-wins, history eviction, datum init/threshold/recenter/policy-off/multi-sink/unregister/explicit-ctor. (5) DatumShift (AxisRotation consumer): asserts the exact convergence angle gamma and velocity/covariance rotation. (6) UERE and OwnShipVelocity: window gating, convergence across sigma range, maneuver suppression + resume, min-sigma floor, velocity output sign. (7) Projection: diagonal covariance at cardinal bearings, heading-in-quadrature, GPS isotropic add. (8) WGS84/Datum round-trips are exact (ECEF-based inverse, so the '30 km round-trip error bound' concern is moot — no small-angle approximation is used; error is machine precision at any range).\n\nNOT FULLY DETERMINED / OUT OF THIS UNIT'S SOURCE SCOPE: The prompt's 'integration-level recenter with occupancy/coastline/static sinks all registered' — those models (core/static, core/land) are outside this unit's source files. The recenter FIRING mechanism (OwnShipProvider) is well tested with CountingSink/TrackShifterSink, and each model's own onDatumRecentered is tested in tests/static/* and tests/land/* individually, but I did not find a single scenario test that wires OwnShipProvider auto-recenter together with TrackManager + occupancy + coastline + static sinks to confirm no cache goes stale in the assembled pipeline; flagging for the synthesis owner of the static/land units. Also not covered anywhere in-scope: southern-hemisphere / negative-longitude datums (all geo/own_ship/datum-shift tests use 53.5N/8E); the math is sign-symmetric so risk is low, but directional (north/east) assertions exist only for the northern hemisphere.
